epigraph.text: You discern my going out and my lying down; you are familiar with all my ways.
epigraph.quote: The residue is the footprint a function leaves at a singularity.

content.text:
The residue is the key to evaluating contour integrals. We define it and 
develop techniques for computing it.

content.title: Residue
content.content:
Let $f$ have an isolated singularity at $z_0$ with Laurent series 
$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a_n(z-z_0)^n$. The **residue** 
of $f$ at $z_0$ is
\[
 \Res_{z_0}(f) = a_{-1},
\]
the coefficient of $1/(z-z_0)$ in the Laurent series.

content.text:
Equivalently, by the formula for Laurent coefficients:
\[
 \Res_{z_0}(f) = \frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_\gamma f(z)\,dz,
\]
where $\gamma$ is any small circle around $z_0$.

content.title: Residue at a Simple Pole
content.content:
If $f$ has a simple pole at $z_0$, then
\[
 \Res_{z_0}(f) = \lim_{z \to z_0} (z - z_0)f(z).
\]
If $f = g/h$ where $g(z_0) \neq 0$, $h(z_0) = 0$, and $h'(z_0) \neq 0$, then
\[
 \Res_{z_0}(f) = \frac{g(z_0)}{h'(z_0)}.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Near the simple pole, $f(z) = a_{-1}/(z-z_0) + a_0 + a_1(z-z_0) + \cdots$

Thus $(z-z_0)f(z) = a_{-1} + a_0(z-z_0) + \cdots \to a_{-1}$ as $z \to z_0$.

For the quotient form: by L'Hôpital, 
$\lim_{z \to z_0}(z-z_0)\frac{g(z)}{h(z)} = \lim_{z \to z_0}\frac{g(z) + (z-z_0)g'(z)}{h'(z)} = \frac{g(z_0)}{h'(z_0)}$.

content.title: Residue at a Pole of Order $m$
content.content:
If $f$ has a pole of order $m$ at $z_0$, then
\[
 \Res_{z_0}(f) = \frac{1}{(m-1)!}\lim_{z \to z_0} \frac{d^{m-1}}{dz^{m-1}}[(z-z_0)^m f(z)].
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.solution:
This is a pole of order 3. Let $g(z) = z^3 \cdot \frac{e^z}{z^3} = e^z$.

$\Res_0(f) = \frac{1}{2!}g''(0) = \frac{1}{2}e^0 = \frac{1}{2}$.

content.text:
For essential singularities, there is no shortcut: we must find the 
Laurent series and read off the $a_{-1}$ coefficient.

marginNotes.text:
Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, 
and you will find; knock, and it will be opened to you.
